title: Managing AI Projects
description: Learn to plan, scope, and execute AI projects — from setting realistic timelines to managing the unique risks and iteration cycles of AI development.
learningObjectives: Plan AI projects with realistic timelines that account for the unique uncertainty, iteration cycles, and evaluation challenges of AI development
Identify and manage the risks specific to AI initiatives — from data quality issues to model behavior drift to stakeholder expectation misalignment
Coordinate cross-functional teams across engineering, data science, design, and legal to deliver AI features effectively

sections.title: AI Project Planning: How It Differs
sections.content:
AI project planning breaks many of the assumptions that work for traditional software development. Requirements are fuzzier, timelines are less predictable, and "done" is a moving target. Understanding these differences is the first step to managing AI projects effectively.

**Why Traditional Planning Fails for AI**

Traditional software features have clear acceptance criteria: the button does X when clicked, the API returns Y given input Z. AI features have probabilistic acceptance criteria: the model produces a good response most of the time, for most inputs, according to most evaluators. This fundamental shift affects every aspect of planning.

Consider **scope**, for example. "Summarize customer emails" sounds simple, but the complexity depends on email length, language, formality, domain jargon, and thread depth. You won't know the true scope until you've tested with real data. **Estimation** follows a similar pattern — an engineer can reliably estimate how long it takes to build a REST endpoint, but estimating how long it takes to get Claude to reliably extract structured data from unstructured text is more like estimating how long it takes to solve a puzzle you've never seen. And unlike traditional features, where something either ships and works or it doesn't, **"done" is a spectrum** for AI. An AI feature ships at 70% quality and improves to 85% over weeks of prompt iteration, so your project plan needs to account for both the initial launch and the improvement trajectory.

**The Phased Planning Approach**

Instead of a single project plan with a single launch date, structure AI projects in phases. Each phase has a distinct purpose and should be treated as its own planning unit:

- **Phase 1: Feasibility (1-2 weeks)** — Can Claude do this task at all? Build a quick proof-of-concept with real data. Test the hardest cases first, not the easiest. The goal is to determine whether the feature is viable, not to build it.
- **Phase 2: Prompt Development (1-3 weeks)** — Iterate on prompts, system messages, and output formats. Establish quality baselines. This phase is where most of the AI-specific work happens, and it's the hardest to estimate.
- **Phase 3: Integration (1-2 weeks)** — Connect the Claude integration to your product's UI, data pipeline, and error handling. This phase is more predictable because it's traditional software engineering.
- **Phase 4: Evaluation and Hardening (1-2 weeks)** — Systematic testing across edge cases, performance optimization, guardrail implementation. Don't skip this phase to hit a deadline.
- **Phase 5: Launch and Iteration (ongoing)** — Ship to users and begin the continuous improvement cycle. Plan for at least 4 weeks of post-launch prompt iteration in your roadmap.

**Estimation Heuristics**

Since traditional estimation methods are less reliable for AI work, you need adapted heuristics. Start by **doubling your initial estimate for prompt development** — if an engineer thinks they can get the prompts working in one week, budget two. The last 20% of quality takes 80% of the effort, and this pattern holds remarkably consistently across AI projects.

You should also **budget roughly 30% of total project time for evaluation.** Testing AI features is more labor-intensive than testing traditional features because you need human judgment to assess quality. Automated test suites can catch regressions, but determining whether an AI output is genuinely "good" requires thoughtful human review.

Finally, **plan for three major prompt revisions.** The first prompt gets you to about 60% quality. The second gets you to 80%. The third gets you to 85-90%. Each revision takes longer than the last because the remaining issues are harder and more nuanced. Knowing this pattern up front prevents the frustration of diminishing returns from feeling like failure — it's simply how AI development works.

sections.title: Risk Assessment for AI Initiatives
sections.content:
AI projects carry risks that traditional software projects don't face. Some of these risks are technical, some are organizational, and some are reputational. A PM who identifies and mitigates these risks early earns trust from engineering and leadership alike.

**Technical Risks**

The most immediate technical risks revolve around the data and infrastructure that underpin your AI feature. **Data quality** is paramount — Claude's output quality is directly tied to the quality of the data you provide. If your knowledge base is outdated, inconsistent, or incomplete, the AI feature will amplify those problems rather than compensate for them. Always assess your data before committing to a timeline.

Another often-overlooked risk is **model behavior drift.** Claude's behavior can change with model updates, meaning a prompt that works perfectly with one model version might produce slightly different results after an update. The mitigation here is straightforward: pin model versions for production features and test new versions before migrating. This adds operational overhead, but it prevents surprises.

**Latency** deserves early attention as well. Claude API calls take time, and if your feature requires sub-second responses while Claude takes 2-3 seconds, you have an architecture problem that no amount of prompt optimization will fix. Test latency early with production-realistic prompts, not short test inputs that give misleadingly fast response times. And keep a close eye on **cost escalation** — API costs are proportional to usage, and if your feature goes viral or gets misused, costs can spike unexpectedly. Implement rate limiting, usage monitoring, and cost alerts from day one.

**Organizational Risks**

Technical risks get the most attention, but organizational risks are just as likely to derail an AI project. The most common is **expectation misalignment** — leadership expects a polished feature by launch, while engineering expects an iterative, improving system. If these expectations aren't aligned early, the project will feel like a failure to someone regardless of outcome. Part of your job as PM is to set the expectation that AI features improve over time and that launch quality is not final quality.

**Skill gaps** present another challenge. Prompt engineering is a new discipline, and your team may not have someone who's naturally good at it yet. Budget time for learning and experimentation, or consider bringing in external expertise for the first project to establish patterns the team can follow on future work.

Finally, watch out for **ownership ambiguity.** Who owns prompt quality? Who monitors output accuracy post-launch? Who decides when to update prompts versus redesign the feature? These questions don't have obvious answers the way traditional software ownership does. Define ownership clearly before the project starts, not after the first quality incident.

**Reputational Risks**

Reputational risks are the ones that keep leadership up at night. **Hallucination in high-stakes contexts** is the most visible — if Claude generates incorrect information and it reaches a customer, the trust damage can be severe and hard to recover from. The mitigation isn't just technical (better prompts, more guardrails). It's architectural: build human review into the workflow before any customer-facing output, especially for high-stakes domains.

**Bias and fairness** concerns apply whenever your feature touches sensitive areas like hiring, lending, healthcare, or education. Claude may produce outputs that reflect biases in its training data, and bias testing in these domains is mandatory, not optional. Similarly, **competitive leakage** is a real concern — be cautious about what data you send to the Claude API. Customer data, proprietary algorithms, and trade secrets flowing through external APIs requires a thorough data security review.

**The Risk Register Approach**

Maintain a living risk register for every AI project. For each risk, track five elements:

- **Probability** — How likely is this risk to materialize? (High/Medium/Low)
- **Impact** — If it does materialize, how severe is the consequence? (High/Medium/Low)
- **Mitigation** — What specific actions reduce the probability or impact?
- **Owner** — Who is responsible for monitoring this risk?
- **Trigger** — What signals would indicate this risk is materializing?

Review the risk register weekly during active development and biweekly post-launch. Risks evolve as the project progresses — new risks emerge and old risks resolve. The register should be a living document that reflects your current understanding, not a checkbox exercise from the project kickoff.

sections.title: Managing AI Development Sprints
sections.content:
AI development doesn't fit neatly into traditional two-week sprints. The iteration cycle is different, the feedback loops are longer, and the definition of "progress" is less clear. Adapting your sprint structure to these realities makes the difference between a team that ships and a team that spins.

**Why Standard Sprints Break Down**

In traditional development, a sprint delivers a set of features that are either done or not done. In AI development, the primary work product is often an improvement in output quality — from 68% to 74% acceptance rate, for example. This creates several problems.

First, **progress is incremental, not binary.** A sprint might deliver a 4-percentage-point improvement in accuracy. That's real, meaningful progress, but it doesn't feel like a shippable unit of work to stakeholders accustomed to seeing completed features in a demo. Second, **debugging is exploratory.** When a traditional feature has a bug, you find the code that's wrong and fix it. When an AI output is bad, you need to analyze the input, the prompt, the context, and the model behavior. This investigation doesn't map to predictable story points because you can't know in advance how deep the problem runs. Third, **evaluation is a bottleneck.** Every prompt change needs to be tested against a representative set of inputs, and this evaluation step takes time. It doesn't parallelize well because it requires human judgment — you can't just run an automated test suite and check for green.

**Adapting Your Sprint Structure**

Rather than abandoning sprints, adapt them for AI work. The structure still provides valuable cadence and accountability, but the contents need to change.

For new AI features, use **exploration sprints** during the first 1-2 sprints of a project. Label them explicitly as exploration so everyone understands the output is a feasibility assessment and quality baseline, not shippable code. This sets expectations appropriately and prevents the team from feeling pressure to ship before they understand the problem space.

Instead of traditional sprint goals like "complete 8 user stories," **track quality metrics as sprint goals.** Goals like "improve acceptance rate from 68% to 75%" or "reduce hallucination rate below 5%" are measurable and meaningful, even if they feel unfamiliar at first. Over time, your team will develop intuition for what a realistic quality improvement looks like per sprint.

When it comes to prompt changes, **batch them rather than shipping one at a time.** Make several changes, test them together against your evaluation set, and ship the batch that improves overall quality. This is more efficient than evaluating every change individually, and it helps you understand how changes interact with each other.

Perhaps most importantly, **reserve sprint capacity for evaluation.** In a typical AI development sprint, plan for 30-40% of engineering time to go toward evaluating and testing changes, not building them. If your team is spending all their time building and none evaluating, quality will silently degrade.

**The Prompt Iteration Workflow**

Establish a repeatable workflow for improving AI quality. This process should become second nature to your team:

Start by **collecting failure examples** — specific inputs where Claude produced poor outputs. Categorize them by failure type: wrong format, missing information, hallucination, wrong tone, and so on. This categorization is important because different failure types have different root causes.

Next, **analyze root causes** for each failure category. Determine whether the issue is prompt-related (fixable by changing instructions), context-related (fixable by providing better input data), or model-related (a fundamental limitation you need to design around). This triage prevents your team from wasting time optimizing prompts when the real problem is upstream data quality.

From there, **propose prompt changes** targeted at the most impactful failure categories. Resist the temptation to fix everything at once — focus on the failures that affect the most users or cause the most harm. Then **evaluate against the full test set**, not just the failures you're trying to fix. This step is critical because fixing one failure mode sometimes introduces regressions in other areas. If overall quality improved without significant regressions, ship the change. If regressions appeared, investigate and iterate.

**Communicating Progress to Stakeholders**

Stakeholders need to see progress, but traditional metrics like velocity and stories completed don't capture AI development progress well. You need alternative ways to make improvement visible.

**Quality dashboards** that show acceptance rate, accuracy, and error rate trends over time are invaluable. A chart showing steady improvement builds confidence even when individual sprints feel incremental. Complement these with **before-and-after examples** — concrete outputs that improved are more compelling than aggregate metrics. Show a bad output from week 1 alongside the improved version from week 4, and the progress becomes tangible.

You can also track a **failure mode burndown** — the number of known failure categories decreasing over time. This gives stakeholders a sense of converging quality and finite remaining work, which is reassuring for teams accustomed to feature-based roadmaps.

sections.title: Cross-Functional Collaboration
sections.content:
AI features touch more teams than traditional features. Engineering builds the integration, design shapes the user experience, data teams provide the content Claude needs, and legal ensures compliance. The PM sits at the center, orchestrating these groups toward a shared outcome.

**Engineering Collaboration**

Your relationship with engineering on AI projects requires more ongoing communication than typical features. Unlike a traditional feature where you write requirements and hand them off, AI features demand continuous back-and-forth as the team discovers what the model can and can't do.

**Prompt engineering is a shared responsibility.** The PM defines what the output should look like — format, tone, content, edge case handling. The engineer figures out how to make Claude produce it — prompt structure, context management, API parameters. Neither can do this alone, and projects that assign prompt work exclusively to one side produce worse results.

Treat system prompts with the same rigor you'd apply to UI copy or marketing language. **Establish a prompt review process** where PMs review and approve system prompts before they ship, just as they review design mocks. System prompts are product-defining artifacts — they shape user experience as directly as the interface itself.

One area that often falls through the cracks is the **fallback contract.** What happens when Claude's API is down, slow, or returns an error? This isn't a technical decision for engineering to make in the moment — it's a product decision that affects user experience. Specify fallback behavior in your requirements alongside the happy path.

**Design Collaboration**

AI features create UX challenges that don't exist in traditional software, and designers need to be involved early to address them thoughtfully.

**Transparency design** is one of the first questions to resolve: how does the user know something was generated by AI? Subtle labels, different visual treatment, or explicit disclaimers each communicate different levels of confidence and trust. This is a design and product decision that directly affects whether users trust the feature.

**Editing and control** patterns matter just as much. When Claude generates a draft, how does the user edit it? Can they regenerate with different parameters? Can they provide feedback that improves future outputs? The editing experience often determines whether users adopt the feature or abandon it after the first disappointing result.

Don't overlook **loading and streaming** patterns. Claude takes time to generate responses, and the choice between streaming text, progress indicators, or skeleton screens affects how users perceive the feature's speed and reliability. Work with design to choose the pattern that fits your feature's context — a real-time chat experience calls for streaming, while a batch report generation might work better with a progress bar.

Finally, **error states** deserve dedicated design attention. When Claude produces a bad output, what does the user see? A generic error message? A prompt to try again with different input? The error experience is often an afterthought, but for AI features it's critical because errors are more frequent and less predictable than traditional software bugs.

**Data and Content Team Collaboration**

The quality of Claude's outputs depends heavily on the quality of the context it receives, which makes data and content teams essential partners in any AI project.

If Claude retrieves information from a **knowledge base** (a RAG architecture), that content needs to be accurate, current, and well-structured. Work with content teams to establish a maintenance cadence — stale knowledge bases produce stale outputs, and users lose trust quickly when they notice outdated information.

You'll also need **evaluation data** — representative examples of inputs and expected outputs to assess prompt quality. Data teams can help assemble and label these evaluation sets, and the investment pays off throughout the project because you'll use them for every round of prompt iteration.

Don't forget about **data pipeline reliability.** If Claude depends on real-time data feeds like user history or a product catalog, pipeline failures will cause feature failures. Coordinate with data engineering on SLAs and monitoring so you're not blindsided when an upstream system goes down.

**Legal and Compliance Collaboration**

AI features often trigger legal review requirements that PMs aren't accustomed to, and the key lesson is to start these conversations early — legal review takes time, and discovering compliance issues late in the project is expensive.

Sending user data to the Claude API may require updated **data processing agreements** or privacy policies. If Claude generates advice or recommendations, you need to clarify **output liability** — who is responsible if the advice is wrong? Legal needs to review customer-facing language around AI-generated content to ensure appropriate disclaimers are in place.

Certain industries like financial services, healthcare, and education have specific **regulatory requirements** about automated decision-making. Confirm your feature doesn't trigger compliance obligations before investing heavily in development. And clarify **intellectual property** considerations early: if Claude generates content based on proprietary data, who owns the output? These questions have nuanced answers that vary by jurisdiction, and legal needs time to research them.

sections.title: Create a Project Plan for an AI Feature Rollout
sections.content: Apply what you've learned about AI project planning by creating a realistic project plan for an AI-powered feature from proof-of-concept through production launch.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM tasked with building a Claude-powered feature that automatically generates personalized onboarding checklists for new enterprise customers. The feature analyzes the customer's industry, company size, purchased product tier, and stated goals (from a sales questionnaire) to generate a customized 30-day onboarding plan with specific milestones and recommended actions.

Your team: 2 backend engineers, 1 frontend engineer, 1 designer, and access to a data analyst. You have 8 weeks until the target launch date.

Create a project plan that includes:

1. **Phase Breakdown** — Divide the 8 weeks into phases (feasibility, prompt development, integration, evaluation, launch). For each phase, specify the duration, key activities, and exit criteria — what must be true before you move to the next phase.
2. **Risk Register** — Identify the top 4 risks (at least one technical, one organizational, and one reputational). For each risk, specify probability, impact, mitigation strategy, owner, and early warning trigger.
3. **Cross-Functional Coordination** — Describe how you'd work with each team (engineering, design, data, legal) during the project. What does each team need from you, and when?
4. **Sprint Structure** — How would you structure sprints for this project? Where would you deviate from standard sprint practices and why?
5. **Stakeholder Communication Plan** — How and when would you update leadership, the customer success team (who will use this feature), and the sales team (who will sell it)?
hints: The feasibility phase should answer: Can Claude generate useful onboarding plans given the input data we have? Test with real customer profiles from your CRM, not hypothetical ones.
A major risk is that the sales questionnaire data may be inconsistent or incomplete — different sales reps fill it out differently. How does this affect Claude's output quality?
Design needs to be involved early to define how the generated checklist is displayed, edited, and shared with the customer. Don't treat design as a polish step at the end.
Legal needs to review any customer-facing AI-generated content — especially if it includes specific recommendations or timelines. Start this conversation in week 1, not week 7.
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A phase breakdown with realistic durations that add up to 8 weeks, where each phase has specific exit criteria (not just 'phase complete') — the feasibility phase should explicitly test with real data and have a go/no-go decision point. (2) A risk register with at least 4 risks covering technical, organizational, and reputational categories. Each risk needs probability, impact, mitigation, owner, and trigger — generic risks like 'project might be late' without specifics are insufficient. (3) Cross-functional coordination that specifies what each team contributes and when — design, legal, and data involvement must start before the integration phase, not after. (4) A sprint structure that acknowledges AI-specific challenges (exploration sprints, evaluation time, prompt iteration cycles) — copy-pasting a standard sprint format without AI adaptations misses the point. (5) A stakeholder communication plan with specific cadences, formats, and content tailored to each group. Deduct points for plans that treat AI development like traditional development, that don't include evaluation time, or that leave legal/compliance review to the last week.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

- Why should you test the hardest cases first during the feasibility phase, not the easiest?
- What's the difference between a traditional sprint goal and an appropriate sprint goal for AI development work?
- Name two risks specific to AI projects that wouldn't appear on a risk register for a traditional software feature.
- Why should legal review happen at the beginning of an AI project rather than at the end?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Managing AI projects successfully requires adapting your planning, execution, and communication practices to account for the unique nature of AI development.

**Plan in phases, not waterfalls.** AI projects need a feasibility phase with a real go/no-go decision, a prompt development phase with quality targets, and an ongoing iteration phase post-launch. Each phase has different goals, different risks, and different measures of success — treat them as distinct planning units rather than steps on a linear timeline.

**Budget for uncertainty.** Double your prompt development estimates, allocate 30% of sprint time to evaluation, and plan for at least three major prompt revisions. AI features get better gradually, not all at once, and your timeline needs to reflect that reality.

**Identify AI-specific risks early.** Data quality, model behavior drift, cost escalation, and expectation misalignment are risks unique to AI projects that won't appear on your standard risk templates. Surface them in week 1, not week 7, and assign clear ownership for monitoring each one.

**Adapt your sprints.** Use exploration sprints for early phases, track quality metrics as sprint goals, and reserve capacity for evaluation. Traditional velocity metrics don't capture AI development progress, so develop new ways to make improvement visible to stakeholders.

**Start cross-functional coordination immediately.** Design, legal, and data teams all need early involvement in AI projects. The temptation is to treat them as downstream handoffs — get the AI working first, then make it pretty, then get it approved. But this approach creates bottlenecks, compliance surprises, and UX problems that are expensive to fix late in the project.
